content.headline: A braise doesn't just cook tough meat — it transforms it, converting the chewy collagen that made it tough into gelatin that makes it silky.
points: Sear the protein hard before braising — you can't brown it later, and skipping the sear means flat flavor throughout
Liquid should come halfway up the meat, not cover it — braising is not boiling
Maintain a bare simmer: a few lazy bubbles, not a rolling boil — boiling tightens meat fibers and makes them stringy
The braise is done when the meat yields to a fork with almost no resistance — not just 'cooked,' but genuinely tender
content.tell_me_more: The magic number for braising is around 160–180°F, held for a long time. Below that temperature, collagen in connective tissue stays tough. Above about 200°F (a boil), the muscle fibers contract forcefully and squeeze out moisture, leaving the meat dry even while the collagen is breaking down. The sweet spot — a long, gentle simmer — slowly melts collagen into gelatin while keeping the fibers relaxed. That's what gives a properly braised short rib or lamb shank its characteristic silky richness: it's not fat, it's gelatin.

missions.title: Braised Chicken Thighs
missions.description: Braise four bone-in chicken thighs — sear them first until deep brown, build a quick soffritto, deglaze with stock or wine, then braise covered on low heat for 35–45 minutes until the meat falls easily from the bone.
missions.why_it_matters: Bone-in chicken thighs are the ideal learning braise: they have enough collagen to benefit from moist heat, the timing is forgiving, and the result is dramatic.
missions.prompt: Show one braised chicken thigh in the braising liquid. The meat should look dark and glossy, and the skin — if skin-on — should have softened and darkened. The braising liquid should be rich-looking, not thin and watery. If you can gently pull at the meat with a fork in the photo, show that it yields easily.
reflection_choices: Meat was genuinely tender and fell from the bone, liquid looked rich and reduced
Meat was cooked through but still a bit firm — needed more time or a lower simmer
Meat was dry or stringy — the braise may have boiled instead of simmered
Didn't get the sear deep enough — the braised meat tasted a little flat and pale

missions.title: Simmer vs. Boil
missions.description: Reflect on how you monitored the heat during the braise and what the liquid looked like throughout.
missions.why_it_matters: Maintaining a bare simmer is the single most important skill in braising — it's also the one most people don't actually do.
missions.prompt: What did the liquid look like during the braise — was it a lazy bubble or something more aggressive? Did you need to adjust the heat during cooking? When you tasted the finished dish, could you tell the difference between what the liquid tasted like when it went in versus when it came out?
